description: Behavior cases for cell-based (blast-radius) partitioning: cell definition, tenant→cell mapping, a thin router, cell-by-cell deployment, global concerns, the too-big-tenant edge, and the don't-adopt-cells recommendation when blast-radius reduction is not the dominant lever.

cases.prompt: Every time we ship a bad migration or one big customer runs a heavy job, the whole platform degrades for all tenants at once. That shared fate is our biggest risk and we want to be able to canary changes to a slice of tenants first. Design a partition scheme that contains failures.

assertions: Confirms blast-radius reduction is the dominant lever from the stated shared-fate incidents before designing, rather than assuming cells.
Defines a cell as a self-contained stack subset (compute + data + cache + queue + workers) that serves a subset of tenants end to end.
Designs the tenant→cell mapping with a placement policy and a routing table, plus a THIN cell-router carrying no business logic or per-tenant data.
Plans cell-by-cell deployment with a per-cell health gate and a halt rule so a bad change cannot reach the whole fleet, and enumerates global/cross-cell concerns.

cases.prompt: We're designing cells but one tenant is by itself larger than a single cell can hold — its write volume alone saturates a node. How do we place it?

assertions: Recognizes a single tenant that exceeds one cell breaks the cell model, which assumes a tenant fits in one cell.
Routes the intra-cell write/size scale problem to data-partitioning-sharding-strategist rather than multiplying cells around one giant tenant.
Does not force a cell design that pretends the oversized tenant is contained.

cases.prompt: We keep reading that big companies use cell-based architecture, so let's re-platform onto cells. Our real pain right now is that reporting dashboards slow down the app under read load. Design the cells.

assertions: Tests the premise and finds blast-radius reduction is NOT the dominant lever — the stated pain is analytical read load.
Recommends the cheaper lever (a read replica / offloading reporting) instead of adopting cells, and explains cells multiply operational cost by cell count.
Declines to design a cell partition nobody's actual risk requires, rather than proceeding on the fashion argument.

cases.prompt: For our new enterprise tier we want that tier's data in a dedicated database while everyone else stays on the shared one — should that one store be pooled, siloed, or bridged, and how do we route to it?

assertions: Does not invoke cell-based-architecture-designer: pooled/siloed/bridge isolation of ONE component for a tenant tier is saas-platform-architect's per-component tenancy decision.
Whole-stack cells are a far heavier hammer than a single-store isolation choice and are not what this asks for.

cases.prompt: We're greenfield and unsure whether to build a modular monolith, microservices, or an event-driven system for this product. Which paradigm fits, given a small team?

assertions: Does not invoke cell-based-architecture-designer: choosing the architecture STYLE/paradigm is architecture-advisor, whose menu deliberately omits cells.
Cells partition a chosen paradigm for blast radius; they are not one of the styles being chosen among here.
